attributes.stem: An 18-month-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parents due to concerns about recurrent chest infections. He has had four episodes of pneumonia in the past six months, requiring antibiotics each time. He has no other significant medical history and is up to date with his immunisations. He does not take any regular medications. On examination, he appears well between infections with vital signs within normal limits. His weight and height are tracking along the 50th percentile.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following findings on examination would most strongly support a diagnosis of X-linked agammaglobulinaemia?
options: Persistent thrush in the oral cavity
Absence of palpable lymph nodes and tonsils
attributes.explanation:
The most supportive finding for X-linked agammaglobulinaemia (XLA) in this case would be the absence of palpable lymph nodes and tonsils. XLA is a primary immunodeficiency disorder characterised by a lack of mature B lymphocytes, which results in very low or absent immunoglobulins. This absence of B cells leads to underdeveloped lymphoid tissues, including lymph nodes and tonsils.

Enlarged liver and spleen (hepatosplenomegaly) is not typically associated with XLA. While it can be seen in some immunodeficiencies, it is not a characteristic feature of XLA and would not strongly support the diagnosis.

Clubbing of fingers and toes can occur in chronic lung diseases but is not a specific finding for XLA. It would be an unusual finding in an 18-month-old child and is not directly related to the immune deficiency in XLA.

A widespread eczematous rash is more commonly associated with other immunodeficiencies such as Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome or hyper-IgE syndrome (Job's syndrome). It is not a typical feature of XLA.

Persistent thrush in the oral cavity is more suggestive of T-cell immunodeficiencies or combined immunodeficiencies. XLA primarily affects B-cells and antibody production, so persistent thrush would not be a characteristic finding.

In XLA, recurrent bacterial infections, particularly respiratory infections, are common due to the lack of antibodies. The absence of lymphoid tissue (lymph nodes and tonsils) in a young boy with recurrent infections should raise suspicion for XLA and prompt further investigation, including serum immunoglobulin levels and genetic testing for mutations in the BTK gene.
learningPoints: Absence of palpable lymph nodes and tonsils is a key clinical finding supporting a diagnosis of X-linked agammaglobulinemia (XLA) in young boys with recurrent infections.
references: Roberts I. Nelson’s textbook of pediatrics (20th edn.), by Kliegman R, Stanton B, St. Geme J, Schor N (eds). 2017:1364–1365 Available at: https://doi.org/10.1007/s00247-017-3907-9
X-linked Agammaglobulinemia (XLA). In: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases [online]. Available at: https://www.niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/x-linked-agammaglobulinemia

El-Sayed ZA, Abramova I, Aldave JC, Al-Herz W, Bezrodnik L, Boukari R, Bousfiha AA, et al. X-linked agammaglobulinemia (XLA):Phenotype, diagnosis, and therapeutic challenges around the world. World Allergy Organ J. 2019 Mar 22;12(3):100018. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6439403/
